---
title: "Columbia River Basin Water Supply Forecasting"
subtitle: "AI Delinquents | ESSD AI Competition 2025"
author: "Cameron Bracken, Stefan Rose, Matt Sturtevant, Amanda Lawter, Jana Simo"
date: today
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, water.scss]
    slide-number: true
    progress: true
    footer: "AI Delinquents | ESSD AI Competition 2025"
    fig-align: center
    highlight-style: github
    transition: fade
    smaller: true
  pptx:
    slide-level: 2
    fig-align: center
---

## The Problem {.center}

**Predict April–September naturalized streamflow volume at The Dalles, OR**

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
**Why it matters**

- Columbia Basin snowmelt drives hydropower, agriculture, and municipal water across the PNW
- April 1 forecasts inform summer reservoir operations
- Can ML improve on existing NRCS/NWRFC statistical forecasts?
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
**Success criteria**

| Metric | Target |
|---|---|
| NSE | > 0 (beat mean) |
| RMSE | < 8,488 kcfs-days |
| Skill score | > 0 vs climatology |

**Target:** Apr–Sep naturalized flow (kcfs-days)
**Forecast date:** April 1
:::

::::

::: {.notes}
Stefan or Cameron — set the scene. The Dalles is the most important control point on the Columbia River. Naturalized flow removes reservoir regulation effects so we're modeling the basin's actual water supply, not what operators decided to release. Our success bar was simply: beat the climatology (historical average) on the held-out test set.
:::

---

## Data & Key Predictors

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="48%"}
**Four sources → 7 features**

| Feature | Source | r |
|---|---|---|
| Apr 1 SWE anomaly (%) | NRCS SNOTEL (16 stations) | **0.79** |
| Jan–Mar mean flow | BPA natural flow | 0.56 |
| Oct–Mar volume | BPA natural flow | 0.55 |
| DJF Nino3.4 | NOAA PSL | −0.46 |
| DJF PDO | NOAA PSL | −0.45 |
| DJF PNA | NOAA PSL | −0.40 |

34 water years (WY 1985–2018)
:::

::: {.column width="52%"}
![La Niña years (blue) produce higher runoff than El Niño years (red) at the same SWE — ENSO modulates snowpack's effect.](presentation_figures/04_swe_vs_target_enso.png)
:::

::::

::: {.notes}
Amanda or Jana — we used naturalized (BPA modified) flow, not observed regulated flow. The scatter plot shows the two key signals: snowpack (r = 0.79) and ENSO phase. La Niña winters enhance PNW precipitation; El Niño suppresses it. This guided our feature engineering choices.
:::

---

## Methodology

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  A[Raw Data\n4 sources] --> B[Clean & QA/QC]
  B --> C[Feature Engineering]
  C --> D{Temporal Split}
  D -->|WY 1985–2012\n28 years| E[Train/Val\nLOYO CV]
  D -->|WY 2013–2018\n6 years| F[Test\nheld out]
  E --> G[Tune XGBoost\noptuna]
  E --> H[Fit MLR]
  G --> I[Evaluate]
  H --> I
```

**Leakage prevention:** Temporal split + hyperparameter tuning inside LOYO folds only. Test set locked until final evaluation.

::: {.notes}
Matt — the key design decision is the temporal split: we held out the 6 most recent years as a true holdout. Leave-One-Year-Out CV within training tuned XGBoost hyperparameters without ever touching the test years. This mirrors how operational forecasts work.
:::

---

## ML Results

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="58%"}
![MLR tracks observations closely; XGBoost prediction intervals (blue band) undercover at ~67% vs 80% target.](presentation_figures/obs_vs_pred_timeseries.png)
:::

::: {.column width="42%"}
**Test set (WY 2013–2018)**

| Model | NSE | RMSE |
|---|---|---|
| Climatology | −0.03 | 8,488 |
| **MLR** | **0.77** | **4,034** |
| XGBoost | 0.46 | 6,170 |

**MLR wins** — with n=34, linear relationships dominate. XGBoost needs more data to exploit nonlinearities.

LOYO CV consistent: MLR = 0.69, XGBoost = 0.44.
:::

::::

::: {.notes}
Cameron — the simple linear model won. Physically reasonable: Apr 1 SWE is linearly correlated with spring runoff (r = 0.79). XGBoost would likely outperform MLR with a longer training record. PI undercoverage reflects mild overconfidence in the quantile models.
:::

---

## Interpretability

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="52%"}
![Antecedent flow conditions top the ranking — early-season runoff integrates temperature, precip, and melt that SWE alone misses.](presentation_figures/feature_importance.png)
:::

::: {.column width="48%"}
**What the model learned**

- **Antecedent flow** (Jan–Mar, Oct–Mar) ranked highest — integrates early snowmelt and base flow
- **Apr 1 SWE** central — both raw inches and anomaly % matter
- **Climate indices** secondary — PDO/PNA/ENSO modulate but don't dominate

**Physical check:** Rankings match operational forecaster intuition. No spurious features.
:::

::::

::: {.notes}
Stefan — the feature importance is physically interpretable. NRCS operational forecasters weight snowpack heavily but use antecedent flow as a quality check. Jan–Mar flow tops the list because by Jan–Mar you have more current information than the Oct–Mar integral captures.
:::

---

## LLM Benchmarking

**Can a general-purpose LLM predict streamflow volume from a text description of predictors?**

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="55%"}
```
You are a hydrologist forecasting seasonal water supply.
Given conditions for a water year, predict the total
April–September naturalized streamflow volume.

Rules:
- Return ONLY: {"prediction_kcfs_days": <number>}
- Typical range: 28,000–71,000 kcfs-days

Water year 2013:
- Apr 1 SWE: 25.2 inches (−12% vs median)
- DJF Nino3.4: −0.43 (La Niña)
- Jan–Mar flow: 103,131 cfs ...
```

- **v1:** Zero-shot
- **v2:** 3 few-shot examples (dry / median / wet)
:::

::: {.column width="45%"}
**Three endpoints tested**

| Model | Params | Port |
|---|---|---|
| Phi-3.5-mini | ~3.8B | 8000 |
| Phi-mini-MoE | ~7B | 8001 |
| gemma-3-12b | 12B | 8002 |

Same test split as Week 3 (WY 2013–2018)

**Parse success: 100%**
Strict JSON worked on every query
:::

::::

::: {.notes}
Matt or Amanda — the core challenge was adapting a classification tutorial to regression. 100% parse success validated the prompt design. We tested zero-shot vs few-shot, then used the better-performing version per model for the final benchmark.
:::

---

## ML vs LLM: Results

![](presentation_figures/llm_vs_ml_comparison_1.png){width=90% fig-align="center"}

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="55%"}
| Model | Type | NSE | RMSE |
|---|---|---|---|
| **MLR** | ML | **0.77** | **4,034** |
| XGBoost | ML | 0.46 | 6,170 |
| gemma-3-12b | LLM | 0.33 | 6,817 |
| Phi-3.5-mini | LLM | 0.12 | 7,824 |
| Phi-mini-MoE | LLM | −0.57 | 10,472 |
:::

::: {.column width="45%"}
**Dominant failure mode:** LLMs regress to the mean — Phi-3.5-mini returned exactly 45,000 kcfs-days on 4 of 6 years

**WY 2017 (wet year):** MLR: 57,557 ✓ · Phi-mini-MoE: 40,000 ✗

Phi-mini-MoE was the **only model worse than climatology**
:::

::::

::: {.notes}
Cameron — the left panel shows the flat-line LLM behavior. Gemma (12B) substantially outperforms the Phi models, suggesting model scale matters more than prompt engineering for this task. Even gemma still beat XGBoost — so a capable LLM can partially compete with a tuned ML model.
:::

---

## Limitations & Risks

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
**ML**

- Small training set (n=28) — limits XGBoost
- Short test window (n=6) — one year swings NSE ±0.15
- PI undercoverage (~67% vs 80% target)
- Stationarity assumption — climate change may shift SWE–runoff relationships

**WY 2015 — worst miss for all models**
Record drought, −51% SWE, strong El Niño.
Every model predicted 30–44k; observed: 31k.
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
**LLM**

- Compressed variance — anchors near climatology regardless of inputs
- Numbers are tokens, not quantities — extremes unreliable
- Not domain-fine-tuned
- Zero-shot better for small models; few-shot better for large — confounds comparison

**Next steps**
Extend training record to WY 1929 · Fine-tune LLM · Sub-basin forecasts
:::

::::

::: {.notes}
Jana or Stefan — WY 2015 is the most important failure operationally — it caught NRCS off-guard too. The LLM numeric reasoning limitation is structural, not a prompt problem. Fine-tuning on BPA/SNOTEL data would likely close much of the gap.
:::

---

## What We Learned {.center}

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
**Modeling**
Simple beats complex at small n — match model choice to data volume

**Data**
Naturalized flow + domain-driven feature engineering were the foundation

**ML vs LLM**
Parse reliability: perfect. Prediction accuracy: not. Scale > prompt design.
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
**From mistakes**
CV score ≠ robustness to extremes — WY 2015 humbled every model

**As a team**
Weekly git branches + reproducible environments + domain review at every step made handoffs seamless
:::

::::

::: {.notes}
All team members — go around for one bullet each. The most valuable learning wasn't the metrics — it was building a reproducible, defensible pipeline and being honest about where it failed.
:::
